messages.content: Make a fake article about John Hughes’ 1980s teen movies released in South Korea this year 2023. Despite of South Korea didn’t have release of any John Hughes’ movies since 1980s. Movies including Sixteen Candles, The Breakfast Club, Weird Science, Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, Pretty in Pink and Some Kind of Wonderful. They would release these movies in DVD and streaming services in Korea. It includes Korean subtitle to understand the language. The Korean dubbed version was on working in progress.

John Hughes' 1980s Teen Movies Released in South Korea This Year 2023

Exciting news for South Korean fans of John Hughes' iconic 1980s teen movies as they hit theaters and streaming services for the first time in Korea. Movies including Sixteen Candles, The Breakfast Club, Weird Science, Ferris Bueller's Day Off, Pretty in Pink, and Some Kind of Wonderful are set to be released in DVD and streaming services in Korea with Korean subtitles for the audience's convenience.

Despite the fact that South Korea did not have a release of any of John Hughes' movies since the 1980s, Hughes' movies have a dedicated fan base in the country, and finally, they will get a chance to enjoy them. These movies have been attributed with inventing the teen-movie genre, focusing on a cast of engaging characters and tackling serious and relevant issues like growing up and the despair of high school.

The Korean dubbed version is planned in the future, but for now, these movies with subtitles can achieve big success among Korean audiences, pushing back the boundaries of cultural and language barriers. However, there will be some inherent cultural differences between South Korea and the United States that South Korean viewers will have to come to terms with about the times of John Hughes, the generation's style of the 80s, and more, but these minor life differences can be bridged by the universal, timeless themes these movies present.

A representative of the media and entertainment industry praises the release of these Hughes movies in Korea as a "big win" and said they are very much thrilled to bring some of the most beloved movies of the 1980s to a brand new, fresh audience. Film buffs and viewers alike will surely enjoy these classic cult films and possibly discover an entirely new sub-genre of teen movies.

Ryu Hye-young and Lee Dong-hwi, who starred in Reply 1988, would join the cast of the Korean dub of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off. Cameron Frye was voiced by Dong-hwi, and Hye-young would voice Jeannie Bueller. In Reply 1988, she played Sung Bo-ra, Deoksun’s mean older sister, while he played Dong-ryo, Deoksun’s friend.

Additionally, Ryu Hye-young and Lee Dong-hwi are set to join the cast of the Korean dubbed version of Ferris Bueller's Day Off, which is still in the works. The well-loved pair, who played siblings in the popular 2015 Korean drama Reply 1988, will put their vocal abilities to the test in the new project.

Lee Dong-hwi, who portrayed the role of Dong-ryo in Reply 1988, will lend his voice to the character of Cameron Frye, Ferris Bueller's melancholic friend. Meanwhile, Ryu Hye-young, the actress famous for her portrayal of Sung Bo-ra, Deoksun's older sister in the same drama, will voice Jeannie Bueller, Ferris's annoyed younger sister.

The upcoming dub promises a nostalgic experience for viewers, as some of the most talented actors on the Korean screen bring one of the most iconic movies of the 80s to life in the Korean language. Fans of the original 1986 film, as well as audiences new to the movie, can look forward to enjoying the Korean-language version of Ferris Bueller's Day Off with the added bonus of listening to the voices of beloved actors Ryu Hye-young and Lee Dong-hwi.
Ryu Hye-young and Lee Dong-hwi reacted to the Korean dub of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off while hearing their voices in movie.

Upon hearing their voices in the Korean dub of Ferris Bueller's Day Off, Ryu Hye-young and Lee Dong-hwi expressed their excitement at being a part of the project.

"I've always been a huge fan of Ferris Bueller's Day Off, so getting to voice Jeannie Bueller in the Korean dub is a dream come true," Ryu Hye-young shared. "It's such an iconic movie, and I'm honored to be a part of it."

Lee Dong-hwi also expressed his enthusiasm, saying, "It was a fantastic experience to voice the character of Cameron Frye. I've always admired the movie and the character, so it's a great privilege to be a part of the Korean dub. I hope that audiences in Korea will appreciate this movie and our dubbing work."

Both actors also shared how much they enjoyed working together again after their roles as siblings in Reply 1988. They said that it was a lot of fun to be a part of such an iconic movie and that they loved working on the project. They both hoped that audiences in South Korea would enjoy the Korean dubbed version of Ferris Bueller's Day Off as much as they enjoyed working on it.
Molly Ringwald, Ally Sheedy, Mia Sara, Anthony Michael Hall, Judd Nelson, Emilio Estevez, Matthew Broderick, Alan Ruck and Jon Cryer reacted to the Korean release of John Hughes’ movies

Molly Ringwald, Ally Sheedy, Mia Sara, Anthony Michael Hall, Judd Nelson, Emilio Estevez, Matthew Broderick, Alan Ruck, and Jon Cryer - all legendary actors who starred in John Hughes’ iconic movies, reacted to the news of the Korean release of his movies with great delight and surprise.

Mia Sara, who played Sloane Peterson in Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, said, “I am thrilled to hear that audiences in South Korea will finally be able to experience Ferris Bueller’s Day Off and the other classic John Hughes movies. They are timeless works that have touched so many hearts around the world, and I am excited to see how Korean viewers will react to them.”

Meanwhile, Anthony Michael Hall, who played Brian Johnson in The Breakfast Club, remarked, “It’s amazing to think that after all these years, The Breakfast Club and John Hughes’ oeuvre are still cherished and appreciated by new generations of viewers. I'm excited to see how audiences in South Korea will react to these timeless movies."

Judd Nelson, who played John Bender in The Breakfast Club, expressed his enthusiasm, saying, "The Breakfast Club holds a sentimental place in my heart, and I am thrilled that it's finally making its way to audiences in South Korea. I hope that these movies continue to spread joy and acceptance around the world.”

Emilio Estevez, who played Andrew Clark in The Breakfast Club, also shared his excitement for new audiences to experience the films. "It’s an incredible honor to be a part of movies that have stood the test of time and continue to resonate with people all around the world. I look forward to seeing how audiences in South Korea will enjoy John Hughes’ work," he said.

Matthew Broderick, who played Ferris Bueller in Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, who had heard of the Korean release along with other actors, said, “It's so exciting to hear that people on the other side of the world will finally be able to enjoy these classic movies. I hope that they will captivate the hearts of new generations of fans.”

The rest of the actors also chimed in and expressed their enthusiasm for the release of these beloved 80s teen movies in South Korea, hoping that it would provide an opportunity for fans in South Korea to experience the magic of John Hughes' work as they did for many years.
Jennifer Grey reacted to the Korean dub of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off and she found out Jeannie’s voice sounded different in Korean.

Jennifer Grey, who starred as Jeanie Bueller in the original version of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, recently reacted to the news of the Korean dub of the movie. She expressed her surprise at hearing that her character's voice would sound different in Korean.

"It's amazing to hear that Ferris Bueller's Day Off is going to be dubbed in Korean and be enjoyed by audiences in South Korea," she said. "But hearing that my character's voice will sound different in Korean is a bit of a surprise. I wonder what Jeannie Bueller's voice will sound like in Korean!"

Despite her surprise at the thought of her character sounding different, Jennifer Grey shared her excitement about the international release of the movie, saying that she hoped it would introduce new audiences to the timeless humor and heart of the film. She also expressed her gratitude that a movie from the 80s that still resonates with viewers 30 years later can now be enjoyed by fans across the world in different languages.

Korean teenagers and young adults influenced by John Hughes’ movies. There is 80s revival in South Korea because of release John Hughes’ teen movies. They can skip school and run like Ferris Bueller.
They copied the outfits from the movies. Teens complaining about their parents because they forgot their birthday. School principals puts troubled and detetioned students to the school library.

The Korean release of John Hughes’ iconic 1980s teen movies has sparked an exciting cultural phenomenon in the country, with teenagers and young adults taking inspiration from their beloved characters and storylines.

The release has led to a nostalgic 80s revival in South Korea, with young people embracing elements of the era - from unique fashion sense to classic hits of the time. In particular, fans of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, are regularly copying Ferris's daring truant day trips around town.

It seems that the humor, relatable characters, and sentimental storytelling of Hughes' movies still speak to the younger generation, bringing renewed attention to 80s cultural influences in Korea. Teenagers are taking inspiration from the classic movies in unexpected ways - for instance, school principals even started putting troubled and detained students to the school library, mimicking the scene from The Breakfast Club, which further proves the influence of these movies.

Interestingly, one such trend is a new type of rebellion where they skip school and run around the city, taking cues from the character of Ferris Bueller in the movie named after him. Some young people have taken to copying the outfits seen in the movies, with many sporting 80s styles and fashions.

Additionally, some have started to copy the characters' witty retorts and rebel behaviors, with teenagers teasing their parents for forgetting their birthdays - much like in the movie Sixteen Candles.

In short, the release of these movies has revealed a whole new dimension to the way South Koreans view 80s popular culture, showcasing how these movies speak to the values and attitudes that resonate with young people today, proving that the timeless themes of Hughes’ work connecting with every generation in any time or place are truly a testament to classic movies.
John Hughes' teen movies were released in the theaters

Along with being available for home viewing, John Hughes' iconic 1980s teen movies have now been released in theaters in South Korea. Fans of these beloved movies can now enjoy them on the big screen, experiencing the humor, heart, and relatable characters they offer in an even more immersive, cinematic way.

The theatrical release of these movies also provides a fresh opportunity for new audiences to experience the magic of John Hughes' work in the company of fellow moviegoers in a shared cultural experience. It’s a great way to relive the 80s and be fascinated by the characters again.

Audiences can now relive the memorable moments of Ferris Bueller's Day Off, The Breakfast Club, Sixteen Candles, Pretty in Pink, and Some Kind of Wonderful in the classic 80s ambiance of the theater screen, and experience them in the way they were originally meant to be seen.

The reaction to this new release has been extraordinary, and many people seem excited and enthusiastic to re-watch the movies and discover the magic of the 80s cinema. The theaters were packed with both the uninitiated and the fervent followers of the genre, making it an unforgettable experience for everyone who missed out on the classic movies when they were originally released in the 80s. 

Overall, the theatrical release of John Hughes' teen movies in South Korea promises to transport attendees back to the iconic era of the 80s in a fun and engaging way, allowing new audiences to discover the magic of Hughes' storytelling and Hollywood of the past.
Korean teens/high school students reacting to the John Hughes' teen movies

Korean teens and high school students have been enthusiastically reacting to the recent release of John Hughes' iconic 1980s teen movies in South Korea. Many of them have been captivated by the compelling storytelling, relatable characters, and light-hearted humor that make these movies so beloved around the world.

For many of them, watching these movies is like taking a fascinating social and cultural journey, exploring American high school life in the 1980s in the company of cinematic role models such as Ferris Bueller, Samantha Baker, and Andy Walsh.

The relatable themes in the movies speak to Korean teens as much as they did to their American counterparts in the 80s, with issues around family, friendships, love, social status, and self-discovery resonating strongly with teenagers everywhere.

Many Korean teenagers also find the fashion and music of the 80s that are portrayed in the movies to be fascinating, inspiring their own fashion and music tastes, as well as some adopting unique elements of 80s fashion to their modern day wardrobes.

In addition, the rebellious nature of some of the characters, like Ferris Bueller and John Bender, has also proven to be a hit, making an impact in Korean glocal culture. A few people have been characteristically inspired by Ferris's day off, even to the extent of taking a nonchalant approach to their school life, symbolizing a refreshing change.

Overall, the reaction of Korean teenagers to John Hughes' teen movies has been overwhelmingly positive, with many of them expressing deep admiration for the way the movies speak to their experiences, values, and attitudes, proving that the timeless themes of Hughes' work still resonate with the upcoming generation outside of his native country.

The cast of Reply 1988 (Lee Hye-ri, Ryu Jun-yeol, Park Bo-gum, Go Kyung-pyo, Choi Sung-won and Ahn Jae-hong) reacted to the John Hughes' movies and Hye-young and Dong-hwi casted in Korean dub of Ferris Bueller's Day Off.

The cast of Reply 1988, including Lee Hye-ri, Ryu Jun-yeol, Park Bo-gum, Go Kyung-pyo, Choi Sung-won, and Ahn Jae-hong, have expressed their excitement and admiration for the recent release of John Hughes' teen movies in South Korea. The actors have been re-familiarizing themselves with the iconic films and are enjoying the 80s revival in Korea.

In particular, they expressed their enthusiasm for the news that other members of the Reply 1988 cast, Ryu Hye-young and Lee Dong-hwi, would be part of the Korean dub of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off. They expressed their pride in their fellow cast members and their involvement in one of the most beloved 80s teen movies.

Lee Hye-ri, who played the character of Sung Deok-sun in Reply 1988, said, “It’s super exciting to see these classic movies from the 80s make their way to Korea. And seeing Ryu Hye-young and Lee Dong-hwi in the Korean dub of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off is a great opportunity for them, and I’m so proud of them."

Ryu Jun-yeol, who played Kim Jung-hwan, shared his excitement at the recent release of these movies in Korea. “I think it's great that people in Korea can watch these classic movies and enjoy them as much as we did," he said.

Park Bo-gum, who played Choi Taek, expressed his admiration for John Hughes' work, saying, "I watched these movies when I was in elementary school. They have a timeless charm that ordinary high school movies just can't replicate."

Overall, the cast of Reply 1988 expressed their appreciation for the cultural impact that John Hughes' movies have made around the world, and the nostalgic 80s revival that they have ignited in Korea, with all of them looking forward to rewatching and reliving the magic of those iconic movies.
Koreans reacting to the release of John Hughes’ movies

Koreans have been reacting with great enthusiasm to the recent release of John Hughes' iconic 1980s teen movies in South Korea. The movies, which have long been beloved by fans around the world, have sparked a new wave of appreciation and admiration among viewers in Korea.

Many people have been captivated by the witty humor, relatable characters, and universal themes that make these movies such timeless classics. The movies have also sparked a renewed fascination with the 80s era, inspiring fans to embrace the unique and eclectic fashion and music of the time.

It has led to various scenes where fans of the movies could be seen celebrating the 80s vibe in various ways, from copying the characters' styles to attending theme parties wearing outfits inspired by the movies.

What is particularly intriguing is how these movies have become freshly relevant in Korea's current cultural and social climate. With a growing sense of social pressure and competition in everyday life, these movie's ability to focus on the emotional troubles of youth that was resonating with Korean viewers, allowing them to connect with the films on a much deeper level.

Many of the themes and messages have also become an inspiration in some way or another as they proved to be relatable to the current generation of teenagers in Korea, as they deal with life's anxieties and anxieties of growing up.

Overall, the release of John Hughes' teen movies has brought a wonderful dose of nostalgia, warmth, and humor to Korean viewers, reminding us of the timeless power of great storytelling and classic memories.
Americans reacted to the Korean release of John Hughes' movies

The American fans of John Hughes’ movies have also reacted positively to the news of their release in South Korea. Fans of these classic movies have been happy to learn that they have been embraced by the Korean audience and that these films retain their enormous cultural relevance across borders and time.

Many fans of John Hughes' work credit these movies for helping them get through teenage years, and their filmography continues to resonate with audiences from different generations around the world. Therefore, Americans are not only excited about South Koreans getting to experience the magic of these classic movies, but they are also glad to see John Hughes' legacy and memory being kept alive.

The teen comedies of John Hughes in the 80s were unique and made a significant impact on the country's culture, as they not only made viewers laugh but also captured their hearts with the insight that they offered into the adolescent mind.
